chapter_info.subject: Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: Adolescence: A Stage of Growth and Change

questions.question: What is adolescence?
questions.answer: Adolescence is the period of development between childhood and adulthood, generally occurring between the ages of 10 and 19 years.
questions.solution: Adolescence is defined as the stage of life that begins around 10 years of age and usually continues until 19 years. It is marked by rapid physical, emotional, and behavioural changes as the body prepares for adulthood.

questions.question: What is puberty?
questions.answer: Puberty is the stage in which the body of an adolescent undergoes external and internal changes to develop into an adult capable of reproduction.
questions.solution: Puberty refers to the phase during adolescence when the body undergoes both visible and internal changes — such as growth in height, voice change, and development of secondary sexual characteristics — that prepare it for reproduction.

questions.question: Name any three secondary sexual characteristics observed in boys during adolescence.
questions.answer: Deeper/hoarse voice, growth of facial hair (moustache and beard), and broader shoulders.
questions.solution: Secondary sexual characteristics are features that distinguish males from females but are not directly involved in reproduction. In boys, these include: (1) deepening of voice due to growth of the voice box, (2) growth of facial hair such as moustache and beard, and (3) broadening of shoulders and widening of the chest.

questions.question: Name any two secondary sexual characteristics observed in girls during adolescence.
questions.answer: Development of breasts and widening of hips.
questions.solution: In girls, secondary sexual characteristics that appear during adolescence include: (1) development of breasts, and (2) widening of hips. Growth of hair in armpits and pubic region is common to both boys and girls.

questions.question: What is the menstrual cycle?
questions.answer: The menstrual cycle is a recurring natural process in girls/women that generally occurs every 28–30 days and is a sign of good reproductive health.
questions.solution: The menstrual cycle is an important internal change that occurs in adolescent girls. It recurs generally every 28–30 days (range: 21–35 days). It is a natural process and one of the indicators of good reproductive health.

questions.question: What are hormones?
questions.answer: Hormones are chemical messengers produced in different parts of the body that regulate growth, development, and various body functions.
questions.solution: Hormones are certain chemicals produced in the body. They are released at appropriate times in response to signals from the brain. They regulate growth, development, reproduction, and various body functions. Some hormones also influence mood and behaviour.

questions.question: What is the Adam's apple?
questions.answer: The Adam's apple is a visible bump in the throat region of boys caused by the growth of the voice box (larynx) during adolescence.
questions.solution: During adolescence, the voice box (larynx) in boys grows larger. This growth may appear as a bump in the throat region, called the Adam's apple. It is responsible for the hoarse or deeper voice in adolescent boys. It is not visible in every individual.

questions.question: What is acne and what causes it?
questions.answer: Acne is a skin condition characterised by small, reddish pimples caused by increased oily secretions from the skin clogging pores during adolescence.
questions.solution: During adolescence, the skin produces more oily secretions. These secretions can clog the pores of the skin and lead to infections, resulting in a skin condition called acne. Pimples are the visible manifestation of acne and are most commonly seen on the face.

questions.question: List the stages of human life in order.
questions.answer: Infancy, childhood, adolescence, adulthood, and old age.
questions.solution: The journey of human life can be divided into five main stages in order: (1) Infancy, (2) Childhood, (3) Adolescence, (4) Adulthood, and (5) Old age. Each stage involves specific changes in the body and behaviour.

questions.question: Name two common harmful substances that adolescents are advised to avoid.
questions.answer: Tobacco and alcohol.
questions.solution: Adolescents are advised to strictly avoid harmful and addictive substances. Two of the most common ones mentioned in the chapter are tobacco (including cigarettes, beedi, gutka) and alcohol. These substances damage physical health, cause addiction, and impair mental functioning.

questions.question: What is menstruation?
questions.answer: Menstruation is the phase of the menstrual cycle during which blood discharge occurs from the body, lasting 3 to 7 days.
questions.solution: Menstruation refers specifically to the phase within the menstrual cycle when blood is discharged from the body. It may last for three to seven days. Some girls may experience pain or discomfort in the lower abdomen during this time. It begins at puberty and ends naturally around the age of 45–55 years.

questions.question: What is substance abuse?
questions.answer: Substance abuse is the regular and uncontrolled use of harmful addictive substances such as tobacco, alcohol, or illegal drugs.
questions.solution: When a person begins consuming harmful substances and develops a strong, persistent urge to use them repeatedly, this is called addiction. The regular and uncontrolled consumption that follows is known as substance abuse. These substances are harmful to both physical and mental health.

questions.question: Distinguish between secondary sexual characteristics and puberty.
questions.answer: Secondary sexual characteristics are the visible features that distinguish males from females but are not directly involved in reproduction. Puberty is the broader stage during which both external and internal changes prepare the body for reproduction.
questions.solution: Secondary sexual characteristics are specific features — like voice change, facial hair in boys, and breast development in girls — that help distinguish males from females but are not directly part of reproduction. Puberty is the overall stage of development during which the body (both externally and internally) transforms into an adult capable of reproduction. Secondary sexual characteristics appear as a part of puberty and signal its onset.

questions.question: Why do adolescent boys experience a hoarser and deeper voice compared to girls?
questions.answer: Because the voice box (larynx) grows much larger in boys than in girls during adolescence.
questions.solution: During adolescence, the voice box (larynx) in both boys and girls grows due to hormonal changes. However, the voice box grows significantly larger in boys than in girls. This greater growth in boys leads to a noticeably hoarser and deeper voice, and may also result in a visible bump in the throat called the Adam's apple. In girls, the smaller growth of the larynx causes only a slight change in voice.

questions.question: A 12-year-old girl notices blood discharge for the first time. What is this process called, and is it a sign of illness?
questions.answer: It is called menstruation, and it is not a sign of illness — it is a natural, healthy biological process.
questions.solution: The blood discharge the girl experiences is called menstruation, which is a part of the menstrual cycle. It typically begins at puberty (around 10–15 years of age). Menstruation is not a disease or illness — it is a completely natural and healthy sign of good reproductive health. It recurs approximately every 28–30 days and lasts for 3–7 days.

questions.question: Why is a balanced diet especially important during adolescence?
questions.answer: Because adolescence is a period of rapid growth and development, the body needs adequate proteins, carbohydrates, fats, vitamins, and minerals.
questions.solution: Adolescence is characterised by rapid physical growth — increase in height, weight, muscle development, and bone strengthening. All of these processes require energy and building materials. Proteins are needed for growth and repair; carbohydrates for energy; calcium and phosphorus for bone growth; iron for blood formation; and vitamins for overall body functioning. Without a balanced diet, growth may be stunted and deficiencies may develop — for example, iron deficiency can cause anaemia, which is particularly common in adolescent girls.

questions.question: What is cyberbullying? How should an adolescent respond if they face it?
questions.answer: Cyberbullying is the use of digital devices to harass others online. An adolescent should not feel helpless but should handle it wisely and seek help from parents and teachers.
questions.solution: Cyberbullying involves using digital devices — such as phones, computers, or online platforms — to harass others by sending misleading messages, spreading false rumours, or sharing someone's personal information without their consent. If an adolescent faces cyberbullying, they should: (1) not feel scared or helpless, (2) not respond impulsively to the bully, (3) save evidence, (4) inform trusted adults such as parents or teachers, and (5) avoid sharing personal photos or information with strangers online.

questions.question: Why is personal hygiene important during adolescence, especially in the armpits and pubic region?
questions.answer: During adolescence, hair grows in the armpits and pubic region, and oily skin secretions increase — making these areas more prone to infections if not kept clean.
questions.solution: Adolescence brings several physical changes including increased oily skin secretions, sweating, and growth of hair in the armpits and pubic region. These changes make the body more susceptible to bacterial and fungal infections if hygiene is neglected. Keeping these regions clean through regular washing helps prevent infections, body odour, and discomfort. For girls, maintaining proper menstrual hygiene using sanitary pads is also essential.

questions.question: List any four emotional changes that adolescents commonly experience.
questions.answer: Mood swings, heightened self-awareness, strong emotions, and increased sensitivity towards others.
questions.solution: During adolescence, emotional changes are a natural part of development. Common emotional changes include: (1) Mood swings — feelings of energy and happiness on some days, and low mood on others; (2) Heightened self-awareness and self-exploration; (3) Stronger and more intense emotions than in childhood; (4) Increased sensitivity and empathy towards others. These emotions can drive positive behaviour, such as taking up social initiatives, or may sometimes cause confusion if not properly understood.

questions.question: How should used sanitary pads be disposed of? Why is proper disposal important?
questions.answer: Used sanitary pads should be wrapped in newspaper and placed in a dustbin. Proper disposal is important for community health and environmental cleanliness.
questions.solution: Used sanitary pads must be wrapped securely in newspaper before being disposed of in a dustbin. This is important for two reasons: (1) Community health — exposed used pads can spread bacteria and infections; (2) Environmental cleanliness — improper disposal contributes to unsanitary public conditions. Biodegradable sanitary pads are also available today, which are more environment-friendly. Proper menstrual hygiene practices reflect responsible behaviour and contribute to the well-being of everyone.

questions.question: Name and briefly describe any two government schemes that promote menstrual hygiene in India.
questions.answer: Menstrual Hygiene Scheme (MHS) and Rashtriya Kishor Swasthya Karyakram (RKSK).
questions.solution: (1) Menstrual Hygiene Scheme (MHS): Launched by the Government of India, it provides sanitary pads free of charge or at reduced prices to adolescent girls in rural areas. It also conducts awareness programmes about menstrual hygiene and health. (2) Rashtriya Kishor Swasthya Karyakram (RKSK): This programme aims to improve the overall health of adolescents, including menstrual health and hygiene. It encourages peer education, where older students help younger ones learn about these important topics.

questions.question: Explain why the variations in timing and extent of physical changes during adolescence are considered normal.
questions.answer: Because each person experiences adolescence at their own pace due to individual biological differences; the timing is controlled by hormones, which vary from person to person.
questions.solution: Adolescence is regulated by hormones — chemical messengers produced in the body. The amount and timing of hormone production varies from one individual to another due to genetic and biological factors. Therefore, some adolescents may begin puberty earlier or later, may grow taller or shorter, or may develop secondary sexual characteristics at different times. For example, one boy may develop facial hair early while another may not notice it until later — both are equally normal. The chapter explicitly states that these variations are completely normal.

questions.question: Why are addictive substances considered particularly dangerous for adolescents?
questions.answer: Because adolescents are naturally curious and easily influenced, making them more vulnerable to trying these substances, which then cause addiction and serious health damage.
questions.solution: Adolescence is a phase of heightened curiosity, excitement, and peer influence. This makes adolescents particularly vulnerable to pressure from peers to try harmful substances like tobacco, alcohol, or drugs. Once tried, these substances are addictive — they create a strong urge to consume them again and again. Over time, this leads to substance abuse, causing serious health issues such as lung damage, breathing difficulties, liver damage, memory loss, and mental health problems. Early exposure during adolescence can also permanently disrupt physical and mental development.

questions.question: Ramesh is 12 years old and has developed pimples on his face. His classmate Suresh, also 12, has not developed any pimples yet. Does this mean one of them has a health problem? Explain.
questions.answer: No. Variations in timing and extent of physical changes during adolescence are completely normal.
questions.solution: The appearance of pimples (acne) is caused by increased oily secretions from the skin, which is triggered by hormones during adolescence. However, hormonal activity varies from individual to individual — some adolescents may experience acne earlier or more prominently, while others may barely develop any pimples at all. The chapter clearly states that the timing, nature, and extent of changes during adolescence vary among individuals and are completely normal. Therefore, neither Ramesh nor Suresh has a health problem; they are simply experiencing adolescence at their own pace.

questions.question: Mary developed a bulge on the front of her neck at age 11 and was prescribed medication and an iodine-rich diet. Manoj developed a similar bump at age 12 but was told it was normal growth. What is the difference between the two conditions, and what does each indicate?
questions.answer: Mary's bulge indicates a thyroid-related condition possibly due to iodine deficiency, while Manoj's bump is the Adam's apple — a normal result of voice box growth during adolescence.
questions.solution: The neck bulge in Mary and the bump in Manoj look similar externally but have entirely different causes: (1) Manoj's bump is the Adam's apple — a naturally occurring prominence caused by the growth of the voice box (larynx) in adolescent boys. It is a normal secondary sexual characteristic and requires no treatment. (2) Mary's bulge, on the other hand, was not a natural part of adolescence. The doctor prescribed medication and an iodine-rich diet, which suggests it was a goitre or thyroid-related swelling caused by iodine deficiency — a medical condition. The doctor advised them differently because one condition is a natural developmental change and the other is a nutritional deficiency that requires medical attention.

questions.question: Shalu claims, 'Adolescence brings only physical changes like growing taller or developing body hair.' Is she correct? Provide a comprehensive correction.
questions.answer: No. Adolescence involves physical, reproductive/internal, emotional, and behavioural changes.
questions.solution: Shalu is incorrect. Adolescence is a multidimensional stage of development that involves far more than just physical changes. A comprehensive description would include: (1) Physical changes: Increase in height and weight, changes in body structure, voice change (Adam's apple in boys), growth of hair in armpits and pubic region, and appearance of acne. (2) Reproductive/internal changes: In girls, onset of the menstrual cycle; in both boys and girls, maturation of reproductive organs. (3) Emotional changes: Mood swings, heightened self-awareness, self-exploration, stronger emotions, increased sensitivity. (4) Behavioural changes: Stronger peer influence, desire for social interaction, increased curiosity, tendency to mimic peers, and deeper interest in new areas. All of these changes together define adolescence.

questions.question: Why is menstruation considered a natural process? What harm can myths and taboos about menstruation cause?
questions.answer: Menstruation is a natural biological sign of good reproductive health. Myths about it cause fear, shame, guilt, and harmful practices like physical isolation.
questions.solution: Menstruation is a completely natural biological process controlled by hormones. It is one of the key signs of good reproductive health in females. It begins at puberty and recurs approximately every 28–30 days until menopause (age 45–55). It is not a disease or a sign of impurity. However, many wrong beliefs (myths) and taboos exist around menstruation, such as the belief that menstruating women are impure or should be physically isolated. These myths are harmful because: (1) They cause unnecessary fear, shame, or guilt in girls. (2) Physical isolation can affect girls' mental health and social well-being. (3) They discourage open discussion about menstrual hygiene, leading to poor health practices. (4) They create stigma around sanitary products. Encouraging a scientific outlook helps dismantle these myths and promotes better reproductive health.

questions.question: How do hormones control the changes during adolescence? Give three specific examples of hormonal effects.
questions.answer: Hormones are chemical messengers produced in the body that are released in response to signals from the brain. They regulate physical changes, reproductive development, and mood.
questions.solution: Hormones are chemicals produced in various glands and organs of the body. They are released at appropriate times in response to signals from the brain, travel through the bloodstream, and act on target organs. Three specific examples of hormonal effects during adolescence: (1) Growth in height: Hormones stimulate bone and muscle growth, causing the rapid increase in height characteristic of adolescence. (2) Onset of the menstrual cycle: In girls, hormones trigger the start of the menstrual cycle at puberty, and continue to regulate it on a monthly basis throughout reproductive life. (3) Voice change in boys: Hormones cause the voice box (larynx) to grow significantly in boys, resulting in a deeper, hoarser voice. Hormones also influence mood and behaviour — explaining emotional changes like mood swings during adolescence.

questions.question: What is the role of peer pressure in substance abuse among adolescents? How should an adolescent respond to such pressure?
questions.answer: Peer pressure can influence adolescents to try harmful substances. Adolescents must firmly say 'NO' and seek support from trusted adults if needed.
questions.solution: During adolescence, the desire for social acceptance and the influence of peers is very strong. Some peers may tempt, persuade, force, or create pressure on adolescents to try harmful substances such as tobacco, gutka, cigarettes, beedi, alcohol, or illegal drugs. Since adolescents are curious and easily influenced, they may give in to such pressure. However: (1) These substances are addictive — once started, it becomes increasingly difficult to stop. (2) They cause serious health damage (lung damage, memory loss, liver damage, etc.). (3) 'Just one time' can easily become a habit. An adolescent should: (a) Firmly say 'NO' the first time and every time; (b) Avoid spending time with people who promote substance use; (c) Seek support from parents, teachers, or counsellors if pressured. The National De-addiction Helpline 14446 (Government of India) is available for help.

questions.question: Categorise the following physical changes as occurring in boys only, girls only, or both boys and girls: (i) Change in voice (ii) Development of breasts (iii) Growth of moustache (iv) Pimples on the face (v) Growth of hair in the pubic region (vi) Growth of hair in armpits.
questions.answer: (i) Boys (more prominent), (ii) Girls, (iii) Boys, (iv) Both, (v) Both, (vi) Both.
questions.solution: During adolescence, some physical changes are gender-specific while others occur in both: (i) Change in voice — Boys (the voice box grows much larger in boys, causing a noticeably deeper/hoarser voice; girls experience only a slight change). (ii) Development of breasts — Girls only (a secondary sexual characteristic specific to females). (iii) Growth of moustache — Boys only (facial hair, including moustache and beard, is a secondary sexual characteristic in males). (iv) Pimples/Acne on the face — Both boys and girls (caused by increased oily skin secretions due to hormonal changes). (v) Growth of hair in the pubic region — Both boys and girls (a common change during puberty in both sexes). (vi) Growth of hair in armpits — Both boys and girls (another common change during puberty in both sexes).

questions.question: Explain the importance of regular physical activity and a balanced social life for adolescents. How does each contribute to overall well-being?
questions.answer: Physical activity keeps the body fit, builds stamina, and improves mood. A balanced social life fosters respectful relationships, responsible use of technology, and emotional well-being.
questions.solution: Regular physical activity contributes to adolescent well-being in several ways: (1) Physical fitness: Exercise strengthens muscles, bones, and the cardiovascular system. (2) Mental health: Physical activity releases mood-enhancing chemicals, reducing stress, anxiety, and mood swings. (3) Stamina building: Regular participation in games and sports builds endurance over time. (4) Social skills: Team sports encourage cooperation and communication. A balanced social life is equally important: (1) Respectful interactions: Being polite and considerate towards others creates a safe and supportive environment. (2) Responsible use of social media: Adolescents must use online platforms carefully — sharing content responsibly, protecting privacy, and avoiding cyberbullying. (3) Emotional well-being: Healthy friendships and social connections help adolescents navigate the emotional challenges of adolescence. (4) Guidance from elders: Seeking guidance from parents and teachers when uncertain helps make responsible decisions. Together, physical activity and a balanced social life lay the foundation for a healthy and joyful adolescence.
